summary: Gruber reviews OS X Mountain Lion as a polished, incremental update rather than a blockbuster release, priced at just $19.99. He argues that Apple's strategy of annual, affordable updates has driven record adoption rates and allowed radical changes to be introduced gradually rather than all at once. The review highlights iCloud document storage as a significant philosophical shift in how users manage files, moving from filesystem-centric to app-centric organization. Gruber contrasts Apple's incremental approach with Microsoft's bet-everything strategy on Windows 8, suggesting Apple's annual cadence borrowed from iOS may be its most important innovation for the Mac platform.
key_points: Mountain Lion continues Apple's trend of lowering upgrade prices ($129 to $29 to $19.99), driving faster adoption than any previous Mac OS release
The most significant changes to the Mac over five years — App Store, sandboxing, iCloud — have been introduced incrementally across releases rather than in one blockbuster update
iCloud document storage represents a radical shift from filesystem-centric to app-centric file management, but Apple is enticing rather than forcing users to adopt it
Apple maintains two distinct systems (Mac OS X and iOS) sharing features through iCloud, while Microsoft bets everything on a single OS strategy with Windows 8
The annual incremental development schedule borrowed from iOS may be the most important thing Apple has brought back to the Mac
money_quotes: It was a wise and practical decision, marketing implications be damned, to devote over a year of engineering effort to under-the-hood OS infrastructure.
A solid technical foundation is good for everyone — users, Apple, developers alike. But it's a hard sell — like opening a restaurant that serves nutritious entrees but no desserts.
But 'faster' isn't fast enough, and the Air now feels like it's getting pretty close to the instant-on wake-from-sleep feel of an iOS device.
Some engineer spent time making copying files in the Finder a more pleasing experience.
Apple's not dragging us to the future; they're enticing us to walk there on our own.
That mindset and development schedule — 'What can we do to make this nicer by next year?' — may well be the most important thing from iOS that Apple has taken back to the Mac.
They were extravaganzas, and they were priced accordingly at $129.
key_insight: Apple's annual incremental update strategy, borrowed from iOS, represents a more sustainable and user-friendly approach to OS evolution than Microsoft's blockbuster release model, allowing radical changes to be absorbed gradually.
